original_abstract: This article examines the exposition of the first commandment within the Heidelberg Catechism (HC), providing a reconstruction of the original 16th-century German and Latin texts. As the primary architect of the HC, Zacharias Ursinus drew significant influence from his mentor in Wittenberg, Philipp Melanchthon. By revisiting two of Melanchthon’s seminal publications, this study uncovers the theological framework and intellectual context that shaped the Catechism’s development. Furthermore, the article analyzes Ursinus’s own detailed treatments of the first commandment found in his *Smaller* and *Larger Catechisms*, as well as insights preserved in his dogmatic lectures. Through this comparative analysis, the research offers a deeper understanding of the HC’s original intent and its enduring theological landscape.
humanized_abstract: The first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism This article presents an exposition of the first commandment as found in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). After a brief reconstruction of the HC1.1 wording in its 16th-century German and Latin sources, the article explores how Zacharias Ursinus, its principal author, was indebted to his Wittenberg mentor and hence reflects the teachings of Philipp Melanchthon, as documented in Melanchthon’s Loci Communes (LW 19.8, 143–69 and 266–96; LCC 19.88, 135–51 and 153–87) and Empfehlung der Heiligen Schrift bei den Konfessionen (LW 19. 370–73). Then, the treatise of the first commandment in Ursinus’ Smaller Catechism 1.29 and Larger Catechism 1.51–55 and his dogmatic lecturers on both Catechisms are taken into account, so as to understand more fully the structure and content of the first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism.
